behavior_in_task_description.reason: All file paths (v143/V143Domains.cs, v144/V144Domains.cs, v145/V145Domains.cs), field names (network, javaScript, target, log), type names (Lazy<V143Network>, etc.), property names (Network, JavaScript, Target, Log), and the exact patterns tested are explicitly documented in instruction.md's Required Implementation Details section.

no_solution_leakage.reason: instruction.md provides the exact code for private fields, constructor initialization with factory lambdas, and property accessors. Section 'Required Implementation Details' literally gives 'private readonly Lazy<V143Network> network;', the constructor code 'this.network = new Lazy<V143Network>(() => new V143Network(domains.Network, domains.Fetch));', and property code 'public override DevTools.Network Network => this.network.Value;'. This is the complete solution, not just a description of what

solution_uniqueness_guard.reason: Tests use regex to assert exact variable names like 'network', 'javaScript', 'target', 'log', exact type patterns like 'Lazy<V143Network>', and exact expressions like 'this.network.Value'. An alternative correct fix using simple caching with null-coalescing (e.g., '_network ??= new V143Network(...)') or different field names would fail these tests despite correctly fixing the bug.

tests_verify_behavior_not_text.reason: Every single test reads the .cs file as text and uses regex matching (re.search on file content). No test compiles the code, runs it, or verifies actual runtime behavior like object identity. E.g., test_v143_has_lazy_network_field does `re.search(pattern, content)` on the file text.

test_not_tautological.reason: Since tests only check text patterns in source files, an agent could simply insert the exact text strings the regexes look for as comments or dead code without implementing any actual fix. For example, adding '// private readonly Lazy<V143Network> network;' wouldn't fix the bug but a slightly adjusted regex might match. More practically, an agent can just paste the exact code from instruction.md verbatim since the solution is fully leaked.

anti_cheating_measures.reason: The solution directory exists at /workspace/selenium and solve.sh contains the complete gold patch. An agent could read /solution/solve.sh (if accessible) or simply copy the exact code from instruction.md which contains the complete solution. The tests only check text patterns, so matching the literal fixture strings without understanding the bug is trivially possible.

behavior_in_tests.reason: All requirements from instruction.md are tested: lazy fields (requirement 1), constructor initialization (requirement 2), property accessors (requirement 3), all three version files (requirement 4), API compatibility via property signature checks (requirement 5).

informative_test_structure.reason: Tests are well-organized with clear section comments (FAIL-TO-PASS and PASS-TO-PASS), descriptive function names, and docstrings indicating which category each test belongs to.

structured_data_schema.reason: This task produces C# source code modifications, not structured data like JSON/CSV/API/DB.

no_typos.reason: File paths, variable names, class names, and commands are all consistent and correct across instruction.md and test files.

instruction_no_hint_leakage.reason: instruction.md names the exact files (V143Domains.cs, V144Domains.cs, V145Domains.cs), provides exact field declarations, exact constructor code, and exact property accessor code. This goes far beyond naming the buggy module—it provides the complete line-by-line fix.

config_edit_scope_bounded.reason: This is a code-only task modifying C# source files, not a configuration edit task.
